title: AI limitation onboarding vs onboarding vs inline message vs warning text vs confidence uncertainty display vs source grounding display vs scope clarification vs human approval gate
summary: Use AI limitation onboarding to teach AI capabilities, limits, data scope, uncertainty, review needs, safe trial, feedback, and fallback before users rely on an AI feature; onboarding for product activation; inline message for local guidance; warning text for a risky action; confidence uncertainty display for reliability of one output; source grounding display for evidence coverage; scope clarification for an ambiguous request; and human approval gate for pausing execution.
decisionRules: Choose AI limitation onboarding when first-time, returning, or changed-capability users need an upfront mental model of AI benefit, capability boundary, cannot do limits, data scope, uncertainty, human review, safe trial, feedback, and manual fallback.

Choose onboarding when the work is product activation, product setup, role selection, sample content, first-value routing, or new-feature orientation without AI-specific limits, data scope, uncertainty, or human responsibility.
Choose inline message when the user is already in a task and needs local guidance or local AI context near one field, generated result, permission gap, or disabled control.
Choose warning text when a risky action has a consequence and needs concise risk copy before the user activates it.

Choose confidence uncertainty display when one AI output needs reliability of one output, probability, alternatives, or low-confidence treatment; use AI limitation onboarding to teach before output exists.
Choose source grounding display when one answer needs source scope, searched sources, retrieved sources, used evidence, unsupported claims, or permission-limited evidence coverage.
Choose scope clarification when the AI must ask a clarifying question before acting on an ambiguous request, missing boundary, timeframe, audience, object set, or action target.
Choose human approval gate when the AI has prepared a high-impact action and execution must pause for pausing execution, approve, reject, edit, or handoff.

AI limitation onboarding should include can do, cannot do, data used, data not available, uncertainty, review required, safe trial, feedback, correction, fallback, skip for now, re-onboarding, and mobile compact states when those values affect trust calibration.

Do not substitute a disclaimer, release note, confidence percentage, citation panel, ask-a-question prompt, or approval dialog for onboarding when users have not yet learned what the AI can and cannot do.
failureModes: The AI is introduced as an expert teammate and users assume human-level judgment, full data access, or final authority.
The product gives a long policy disclaimer but no task-specific examples, safe trial, feedback, or fallback.
Users discover data gaps only after acting on a generated answer.
A confidence badge or source list appears without explaining what tasks the AI is unsuitable for.
A new autonomy or data-use change ships without re-onboarding returning users.
The AI asks clarification questions or pauses for approval, but users were never told why those moments occur.

sources.note: Supports AI onboarding around benefits, limits, safe exploration, co-learning, and human-like expectation risk.
Supports trust calibration, uncertainty, partial explanations, and user judgment.
Supports limitation failures, missing data, low confidence, feedback, and manual alternatives.
Supports transparency about AI capabilities, limitations, safety, and informed decisions.
Supports transparency around capabilities, limitations, system context, and performance behavior.
